coredata.dc:title: Simple and sensitive fluorescence sensor for methotrexate detection based on the inner filter effect of N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yanan
Zou, Shiying
Huo, Danqun
Hou, Changjun
Yang, Mei
Li, Junjie
Bian, Minghong
coredata.dc:description: Reliable and simple detection methods for chemotherapeutic agent contaminants, such as methotrexate, are required to minimize their possible toxic threat. However, detecting them remains a challenging in environmental and biological analysis. Here, we developed a fluorescent N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S co-doped CQDs) probe to detect methotrexate on the basis of the inner filter effect of fluorescence. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescent probe exhibits high sensitivity and specific selectivity with a linear detection range from 0.4 μg/mL to 41.3 μg/mL and a low detection limit of 12 ng/mL (S/N = 3). Further, this N, S co-doped CQDs fluorescent probe can be used to analyze extracellular fluids and wastewater samples satisfactorily, thereby showing a remarkable potential for broad applications in biological molecule determination and environmental analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methotrexate
N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots
Inner filter effect
Fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped CQDs
Detection of methotrexate in wastewater samples and extracellular fluids
Optical properties of the obtained N, S co-doped CQDs
Analytical performance of the N, S co-doped CQDs probe for methotrexate
Selectivity of the N, S co-doped CQDs probe for methotrexate detection
Methotrexate detection in real samples and comparison with HPLC assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) AFM image of as-prepared N, S co-doped CQDs on freshly cleaved mica. Size: 1.2×1.2 μm. (B) The height profile along the line. (C) HRTEM image and (D) XRD pattern of as--prepared N, S co-doped CQDs.
(A) Full scan XPS spectrum of N, S co-doped CQDs. (B–D) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, and S 2p.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared N, S co-doped CQDs. Inset: the photographs of N, S co-doped CQDs aqueous solution under UV light of 312 nm (1) and visible light (2). (B) Emission spectra of N, S co-doped CQDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 400 nm. Inset: The normalized FL emission spectra of N, S co-doped CQDs. (C) Maximum fluorescence excitation (I) and emission (II) spectra of N, S co-doped CQDs; UV–vis absorption spectrum of methotrexate in 50 mM potassium hydrogen phthalate (pH = 4) buffer solution (III).

Effect of pH (A), different buffer mediums (B) (1:0.1MHAc-0.1M NaAc; 2: 50 mM potassium hydrogen phthalate; 3:0.1M glycine-0.1MKCl-0.1MHCl; 4:50 mM succinic acid-50 mM sodium borate; 5:0.1M lactic acid-0.1M sodium lactate), buffer medium volume of 50 mM potassium hydrogen phthalate (C), incubation temperature (D), and incubation time (E).

(A) Emission spectra of N, S co-doped CQDs in the presence of different concentration of methotrexate in 50 mM potassium hydrogen phthalate buffer solution (pH = 4). The concentration of methotrexate from the top to the bottom: 0, 0.4, 0.8, 2.5, 4.1, 8.3, 16.5, 24.8, 33 and 41.3 μg/mL. (B) Linear plots of the N, S co-doped CQDs fluorescence versus methotrexate concentration.

(A) Zeta potential of N, S co-doped CQDs in 50 mM potassium hydrogen phthalate (pH = 4) buffer solution. (B)Time-resolved decay of the N, S co-doped CQDs with methotrexate (33 μg/mL) in 50 mM potassium hydrogen phthalate (pH = 4) buffer solution. (C) Cyclic voltammogram of the N, S co-doped CQDs in 50 mM potassium hydrogen phthalate (pH = 4) buffer solution. (D)The LUMO and HOMO of N, S co-doped CQDs and methotrexate.
Schematic illustration of the sensitive fluorescence detection of methotrexate based on N, S co-doped CQDs.
Determinations of methotrexate in wastewater samples and extracellular fluids of A549 cells.

head.ce:title: Simple and sensitive fluorescence sensor for methotrexate detection based on the inner filter effect of N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biorheological Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, State and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Vascular Implants, Bioengineering College of Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400044, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biorheological Science and Technology of Ministry of Education
State and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Vascular Implants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical and Testing Center, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong, 643000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liquor Making Biology Technology and Application of Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, College of Bioengineering, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong, 643000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liquor Making Biology Technology and Application of Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province
Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yanan Zhao and Shiying Zou contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reliable and simple detection methods for chemotherapeutic agent contaminants, such as methotrexate, are required to minimize their possible toxic threat. However, detecting them remains a challenging in environmental and biological analysis. Here, we developed a fluorescent N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S co-doped CQDs) probe to detect methotrexate on the basis of the inner filter effect of fluorescence. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescent probe exhibits high sensitivity and specific selectivity with a linear detection range from 0.4 μg/mL to 41.3 μg/mL and a low detection limit of 12 ng/mL (S/N = 3). Further, this N, S co-doped CQDs fluorescent probe can be used to analyze extracellular fluids and wastewater samples satisfactorily, thereby showing a remarkable potential for broad applications in biological molecule determination and environmental analysis.

A simple and sensitive fluorescence sensor was constructed based on the inner filter effect of N, S co-doped carbon dots for methotrexate detection.
Image 1

ce:keyword.ce:text: N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: According to the latest report of the World Health Organization (WHO), cancer is the main cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide. The high incidence of cancer leads to the increasing consumption of chemotherapeutic agents, because chemotherapy is among the most commonly used treatment options [1,2]. One of the chemotherapeutic agents or cancer medication is methotrexate, which has been recognized as a folate antagonist to inhibit dihydrofolate reductase, reduce DNA synthesis, and decrease cell mitosis and proliferation [3,4]. It is also widely used to treat acute lymphoblastic leukemia, breast carcinoma, and other malignant tumors, as well as various disorders, such as rheumatoid arthritis and lupus [5,6]. However, as a cytotoxic or antineoplastic agent, methotrexate not only acts on neoplastic cells but also attacks healthy cells and tissues, possibly resulting in alopecia, bone marrow suppression and potentially life-threatening pneumonitis and cirrhosis [3,7]. Methotrexate contamination has been found in the effluent of hospitals and sewage treatment plants [8]. The presence of chemotherapeutic agents in the environment causes a possible detrimental impact on human health and ecological safety. Therefore, chemotherapeutic agent contamination in both biological and environmental samples should be monitored.

Conventional approaches for methotrexate determination include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) in tandem with ultraviolet visible [9–11], mass spectrometric detection [11–16], spectrophotometry coupling with derivatization [17–20], electroanalysis [21–24], surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [3], and immunoassays [25]. In comparison with these physicochemical methods, fluorescent spectroscopy is considered a promising strategy because it is simple, easily operable, and free from complicated pretreatment processes. Conventional fluorescence methods for detecting methotrexate also need complicated derivatization to convert methotrexate into fluorescent compounds. Conversely, nanomaterial fluorescent probes can offer a simple method for determining methotrexate in accordance with the principle of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), or electron transfer. To the best of our knowledge, limited studies on nanomaterial fluorescent probes for methotrexate detection have been conducted. Wang et al. synthesized N,S-co-doped fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) for detecting methotrexate [26]. Chen et al. fabricated gold nanoclusters as fluorescence probes that yield satisfactory results for determining methotrexate in real samples [27]. However, the linear detection range of these fluorescent probes and their quenching mechanism should be further improved. Therefore, novel fluorescent probes for methotrexate detection should be explored.

In comparison with conventional emissive materials, such as organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs are good candidates because of their stable photoluminescence, low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, good water solubility, and chemical inertness [28–36]. However, the relatively low fluorescent quantum yield (FLQY, usually less than 50%) and the self-quenching problem of CDs restrict their applications in bioanalysis. Doped CDs, such as transition metal-doped CDs or co-doped CDs, were proposed to have a good performance in fluorescence. In comparison with transition metal-doped CDs, nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) doped CDs are promising because of their robust chemical inertness, low toxicity, and simple preparation. In this study, we successfully prepare N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S co-doped CQDs) by using citric acid and L-cysteine as C, N and S sources through a simple hydrothermal route. The prepared N, S co-doped CQDs can be easily quenched by methotrexate on the basis of IFE. Although IFE is originally a source of errors in fluorimetry and should be avoided [37,38], IFE-based assays do not require covalent linking between an absorber and a fluorophore or only work when these two components are at a particular distance or have a complementary geometry [39–41]. Hence, IFE offers considerable flexibility and simplicity in probe fabrication and experimental design. Moreover, the IFE of fluorescence has emerged as an efficient strategy to design and develop novel probes for the detection of variable analytes by choosing suitable absorber fluorophore pairs. In comparison with previously reported IFE-based strategies that comprise a fluorophore, an absorber and a target analyte [42–46], the strategy proposed in our work involves two compositions: a fluorophore and an absorber. Our approach is simpler and more efficient than conventional methods because a target analyte acts as an absorber in our IFE system. Furthermore, a series of studies is performed to demonstrate the IFE principle of fluorescence quenching. Our experimental results show that the fluorescent chemosensor exhibits excellent analytical performance under mild conditions with high sensitivity and comparable accuracy, and can be successfully used to detect methotrexate in wastewater and extracellular fluids samples.

L-cysteine was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Quinine sulfate dehydrate was obtained from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000Da was obtained from Shanghai Green bird Science and Technology Development Co. (Shanghai, China). RPMI-1640 medium and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Shanghai ExCell Biology, Inc. HPLC grade acetonitrile was obtained from Fisher Scientific (Belgium). Methotrexate assay standard and all other chemicals (analytical-reagent grade) were acquired from Aladdin Chemistry Reagent Company (Shanghai, China) without further purification. Deionized water (18.2 MΩ) used in all experiments was generated by a Millipore purification system (Millipore, France).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: N, S co-doped CQDs were prepared in accordance with previously described methods [47] with minor modifications. Citric acid monohydrate (2.0 g) and L-cysteine (1.0 g) were dissolved in 5 mL of water, and evaporated at 70 °C for 12 h. The resultant thick syrup was heated hydrothermally in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The resultant black syrup neutralized with 1 M NaOH solution, diluted to 100 mL, and then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was collected, dialyzed using a membrane with molecular weight cutoff of 1000 for 24 h, and kept in the dark for future use.

Fluorescence spectra were measured with the fluorescence spectrometer PerkinElmer LS55 (PE, USA). Absorption spectra were recorded by TU–1901 double-beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (Peking General Instrument Co. Ltd., Beijing, China). The morphologies and microstructure of the as-prepared material was characterized by A JEM–2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were performed in ambient condition using a MFP–3D AFM (Asylum Research, Santa Barbara. CA). Pt/Cr coated silicon tips (Multi 75E–G Budget sensor) with a spring constant of k∼2–5N/m and a resonance frequency at ∼60–70 kHz were used for imaging. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT–IR) were recorded on a PerkinElmer FT/IR–NIR spectrometer Frontier (USA). Zeta potential was measured using a zetasizer (Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments, UK). X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were conducted using a Kratos XSAM800 X–ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, UK). The crystal structures of the N, S co-doped CQDs were identified by powder X–ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker/D2 PHASER) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). Luminescence lifetime was determined on an Edinburgh analytical instrument (FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer). Electrochemical measurement was performed on CHI660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instrument Company, China) in the electrolyte containing 50 mM potassium hydrogen phthalate buffer solution (pH = 4) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The HPLC system was consisted of an Agilent LC1200 liquid chromatograph (Agilent, USA), a DAD detector and a Gemini C18 column (250×4.60 mm id., phenomenex, USA) with a data acquisition software (Agilent, USA). The operation conditions followed pharmacopoeia of China (2010): the mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile–7.0% citric acid solution–2% anhydrous disodium hydrogen phosphate (8.5:10:80, pH = 6); flow rate, 1.0 mL/min; injection volume, 10 μL; detection wavelength, 302 nm.

Fluorescence was recorded at emission peak of around 450 nm under excitation of 355 nm. The excitation slit width was set to 15.0 nm with an emission slit width of 8 nm. Methotrexate was detected in accordance with the following predetermined procedure. A stock solution of N, S co-doped CQDs (100 μL) was added to 2.0 mL of potassium hydrogen phthalate buffer solution (50 mM, pH = 4). Different volumes of methotrexate work solutions or sample solutions were successively pipetted. The final volume of the mixture was adjusted to 8 mL with deionized water by using a vortex mixer. The mixture was incubated at room temperature for 5 min and subjected to fluorescent measurement. All fluorescent measurements were repeated at least three times to obtain average intensities for further data analysis. F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensities of the N, S co-doped CQDs in the presence and absence of methotrexate, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of methotrexate in wastewater samples and extracellular fluids
ce:section.ce:para: Wastewater samples were filtered with 0.22 μm microporous membranes to remove particulate matters. For protein precipitation, 2 mL of acetonitrile and 2 mL of water were added to 1 mL of supernatant. This mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 30 min. The as-obtained supernatant samples were stored at 4 °C before they were analyzed.

Non-small cell lung cancer cells (A549 cells) were seeded in 5 cm × 5 cm flasks and cultured in 1640 medium containing 10% FBS supplemented with 100 units/mL penicillin and 100 mg/mL streptomycin in an incubator at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. The extracellular fluid of A549 cells was collected and centrifuged for 10 min at 1000 rpm. The protein in the extracellular fluid samples was precipitated before it was stored in accordance with the procedures similar to those for wastewater samples. The Obtained supernatant samples were stored at 4 °C before they were examined.

Fig. 1 . shows the morphological characteristics and microstructure through high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image and AFM. The majority of the particles have a mean diameter of 5 nm, and display a lattice spacing distance of 3.4 Å (Fig. 1C), and resemble those of graphite (002) facets. In Fig. 1A and B, the thickness of N, S co-doped CQDs ranges from 0.4 nm to 1.0 nm, indicating that they comprise one to three graphene layers. Consistent with HRTEM results (Fig. 1D), the XRD pattern of the N, S co-doped CQDs shows a broad diffraction peak at 23.9° (d = 0.34 nm). XRD and HRTEM reveal the crystalline core with an amorphous surface of N, S co-doped CQDs.

The surface functional groups and the chemical composition of the prepared N, S co-doped CQDs are investigated. The FT–IR spectrum of the as-prepared N, S co-doped CQDs is illustrated in Fig. S1. The broad absorption band at 3432 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, indicating the abundance of amino and hydroxyl groups on the N, S co-doped CQD surface, which is beneficial to the hydrophilic properties of N, S co-doped CQDs. A sharp decline in intensity of S–H peak at 2552 cm−1 compared with that of L-cysteine indicates the formation of aromatic heterocycles and the reduction of –SH. The peak of C=O at 1702 cm−1 shifts to a wave number that is lower than that of the peak of citric acid, further supporting the presence of aromatic π structure. The peak at 1388 cm−1 is attributed to the C–N, whereas peak at 1078 cm−1 is associated with the C=S vibration and the weak C–S stretching vibration at 637 and 571 cm−1, respectively.

The full scan XPS spectrum of N, S co-doped CQDs (Fig. 2 A) reveals three peaks at 534, 399.4, and 287.5 eV, which correspond to O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s, respectively. Two peaks at 231 and 167 eV are possible signs of S 1s and S 2p respectively, demonstrating the successful doping of N and S in the co-doped CQDs. The high resolution scan of the C 1s region with three different peaks (Fig. 2B) implies that carbon is in three different chemical states. The peak near 284.6 eV suggests the presence of graphitic structure (sp2 C=C) in N, S co-doped CQDs. The peak at around 285.6 eV can be attributed to sp3-bonded carbon with oxygen (C–O), sulfur (C–S), and nitrogen (C–N), whereas the peak at 287.9 eV can be assigned to C=O. The N 1s spectrum shows two peaks (Fig. 2C) at 400.3 and 399.1 eV, which correspond to pyrrolic N (C–N–C) bands. The High-resolution spectrum of the S 2p in N, S co-doped CQDs clearly exhibits three peaks (Fig. 2D). Among them, the peak at 163.6 and 166.4 eV respectively represent S 2p3/2 S–C and S–O, whereas the peak at 164.3eV is associated with S 2p3/2 of thiophene. This observation is consistent with the FT–IR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the obtained N, S co-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV-vis absorption and fluorescent measurement in Fig. 3 show the optical properties of the as-obtained N, S co-doped CQDs. The UV-vis spectrum includes two characteristic absorption peaks at around 209 nm and 347 nm (Fig. 3A). At an excitation of 209 nm, no clear FL signal appears because of the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, indicating the existence of aromatic heterocycles. This finding also agrees well with the FT-IR analysis. At excitation of 345 nm, a strong emission is observed because of the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states. Fig. 3A (inset) illustrates a bright blue fluorescence under UV light illumination (312 nm) at a low N, S co-doped CQD concentration (10 μg/mL). No fluorescence can be detected in the L-cysteine and citric acid solutions at 312 nm, suggesting that the bright blue fluorescence originates from the N, S co-doped CQDs. In Fig. 3B, the emission wavelength of N, S co-doped CQDs is excitation-dependent. When the excitation wavelength is changed from 300 nm to 400 nm, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are found at 355 nm and 450 nm respectively with a Stokes shift of 95 nm (Fig. 3C). Therefore, 355 nm and 450 nm are selected as the respective excitation and emission wavelengths for the following experiments. The relative PLQY of N, S co-doped CQDs is as high as 57.2% in reference to quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54 at 355 nm) dissolved in H2SO4 (0.1 M, refractive index 1.33), indicating that the strong quantum confinement of nanoparticles is beneficial to highly sensitive materials for chemosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the N, S co-doped CQDs probe for methotrexate
ce:section.ce:para: Several related factors, including pH, buffer medium, volume of buffer solution, incubation temperature, and time of the fluorescence quenching of N, S co-doped CQDs are investigated. In Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence quenching of N, S co-doped CQDs is pH dependent and relatively low under neutral and alkali conditions. By contrast, the fluorescence quenching of N, S co-doped CQDs reaches the maximum and remains stable in weakly acidic solutions at pH = 4. The maximum fluorescence quenching is observed in 50 mM potassium hydrogen phthalate buffer solution among the other buffer media used in this study (Fig. 4B). Therefore, this buffer solution is preferred. The optimized buffer medium volume, incubation temperature and reaction time are set at 2 mL, room temperature (25 °C), and 5 min, respectively (Fig. 4C, D and E).

Under the optimized conditions, methotrexate samples with different concentrations are mixed with N, S co-doped CQDs to evaluate the analytical performance. Fig. 5 indicates that the fluorescence quenching of N, S co-doped CQDs toward methotrexate occurs at a concentration between 0.4 and 41.3 μg/mL. This result fits well with the Stern-Volmer type equation F0/F = 1.0392 + 0.0015C with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.998. The limit of detection (LOD) is 12 ng/mL at signal/noise ratio of 3. This as-developed fluorescent probe is comparable with that reported previously (Table S2), suggesting that N, S co-doped CQDs can be successfully applied to monitor methotrexate in real time.

A series of studies is carried out in the follow-up experiments to further understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism of methotrexate toward the as-prepared N, S co-doped CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3C, the UV−vis absorption spectrum of methotrexate in 50 mM potassium hydrogen phthalate (pH = 4) buffer solution shows a characteristic absorption peak at 372 nm, which overlaps with the excitation band of N, S co-doped CQDs well. This suggests that fluorescence quenching may be related to FRET, IFE, and electron transfer mechanisms [48]. In our study, the NH2-groups of methotrexate are protonated and positively charged in 50 mM potassium hydrogen phthalate (pH = 4) buffer solution because pKa of methotrexate is 4.5–5.8. The zeta potential of N, S co-doped CQDs is 20.5 mV (Fig. 6 A). The strong electrostatic repulsion effect likely prevents methotrexate from being adsorbed on the surface of N, S co-doped CQDs due to charge repulsion. Therefore, an effective FRET donor acceptor assembly is unavailable in the present system [49]. In addition, fluorescence lifetime, which remains almost unaffected by IFE, fluorophore concentration, and static quenching, may provide evidence to confirm the absence of FRET, because non-radiative energy transfer is expected to substantially alter the exciton lifetime of a donor. In Fig. 6B, the average lifetime of N, S co-doped CQDs is hardly changed in the presence of methotrexate, suggesting that the quenched emission of N, S co-doped CQDs by methotrexate is not in accordance with the FRET principle between N, S co-doped CQDs and methotrexate. Afterward, the energy levels of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (ELUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (EHOMO) of N, S co-doped CQDs and methotrexate are investigated, respectively. EHOMO and ELUMO of N, S co-doped CQDs can be estimated with the following empirical formula [48,50]:
(1) EHOMO = −e (Eox + 4.4)
(2) ELUMO = −e (Ered + 4.4)
where Eox and Ered are the onsets of oxidation and reduction potential of N, S co-doped CQDs, respectively. Ered is −0.357 eV measured by cyclic voltammograms (Fig. 6C). Thus, the corresponding ELUMO is around −4.043 eV. EHOMO cannot be obtained because of the irreversible oxidation behavior, but it can be determined by combining the Eg with the optical energy band gap [51], where Eg is a result of the absorption edge in the absorption spectrum of N, S co-doped CQDs.
(3) EHOMO = ELUMO−Eg

Eg is 3 eV, so EHOMO is calculated to be −7.04 eV. The respective ELUMO and EHOMO of methotrexate are −0.4114 eV and −3.7998 eV in accordance with density functional theory approach at B3LYP hybrid density functional level. Photoexcited electrons in N, S co-doped CQDs are not energetically allowed to undergo electron transfer from the LUMO of the N, S co-doped CQDs to the LUMO of methotrexate, thereby excluding the electron transfer between N, S co-doped CQDs and methotrexate (Fig. 6D). Therefore, IFE is the major mechanism responsible for the fluorescence quenching of N, S co-doped CQDs.

On the basis of the experimental results, we conclude that N, S co-doped CQDs can interact with methotrexate through two kinds of interactions: 1) hydrogen bonding between –OH, –NH2, and –COOH at the surface of N, S co-doped CQDs and methotrexate; 2) π-π stacking between the aromatic heterocyclic part of N, S co-doped CQDs and the pteridine ring or phenyl group of methotrexate. With these specific interactions, N, S co-doped CQDs strongly target methotrexate to serve as chemosensor for the determination of methotrexate based on IFE. The possible mechanism of the proposed method is presented in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the N, S co-doped CQDs probe for methotrexate detection
ce:section.ce:para: The complexity of environmental and biological samples poses a major challenge for the detection of chemotherapeutic agents. Thus, selectivity experiments are performed in this study. The fluorescence intensities of N, S co-doped CQDs are analyzed in the presence of the following group of possible interfering counterparts and a series of drugs: some cations (e.g., K+, Na+, NH4 +, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, and Fe3+), anions (e.g., SO4 2− NO3 −, F−, Cl−, and Br−), glucose, starch, bovine serum albumin, dopamine, uric acid, folic acid, and adrenaline. Most chemicals can be allowed to coexist at a concentration of greater than the 10-fold of methotrexate with a relative error of less than 5.0% (Table S1). The concentrations of these interfering substances are relatively low in real samples, so these substances cannot interfere with the responses of methotrexate determination. The major substance causing interference is folic acid, which can also quench the fluorescence of N, S co-doped CQDs because the chemical structure and UV-vis absorption spectrum of folic acid are similar to those of methotrexate. However, folic acid does not disturb methotrexate analysis at a tolerance ratio less than 0.5, probably because of the different sensitivities of the as-prepared fluorescent probe for methotrexate and folic acid. These results suggest that N, S co-doped CQDs can be used as a fluorescent probe for methotrexate in biological and environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methotrexate detection in real samples and comparison with HPLC assay
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the developed method is tested using methotrexate spiked in the effluent of local tumor hospital and the extracellular fluids of A549 cells. The obtained recoveries of the samples spiked with standard methotrexate solutions (6 and 33 μg/mL) vary from 96.67% to 103.33%. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of each sample is below 4.63%, indicating high reproducibility and precision (Table 1 ). Thus, the proposed method is feasible for the detection of methotrexate in environmental and biological samples.

In accordance with the Pharmacopoeia of China (2010), HPLC is performed for the detection of methotrexate in the spiked effluent of local tumor hospital and the extracellular fluids of A549 cells to compare and investigate the performance of the proposed IFE-based fluorescent detection method. In accordance with the standard working curve between the peak area and the methotrexate concentration (Fig. S2B), the recovery of methotrexate can be obtained in the range of 94.57% %–101.51% with the variation coefficient of 1.58%–3.85% (Table 1). T-test analysis (confidence level 95%) reveals no significant difference between the two methods. The proposed fluorescent probe offers a good alternative for the rapid detection of methotrexate in related environmental and biological samples because of its apparent advantages, such as high sensitivity, simple operation, low cost, time saving, and convenient analysis.

In summary, N, S co-doped CQDs with high FLQY are successfully prepared using citric acid and L-cysteine as C, N, and S sources through a simple hydrothermal route. They are also successfully demonstrated as a fluorescent probe to detect methotrexate on the basis of IFE of fluorescence. In comparison with conventional IFE-based strategies comprising a fluorophore, an absorber, and a target analyte, the present strategy involves only two compositions: a fluorophore and an absorber. In present approach, the target analyte is the absorber in the IFE system, thereby making this approach simpler, more efficient, and superior to conventional strategies [52]. Thus, the proposed fluorescent probe based on the as-prepared N, S co-doped CQDs can serve as a high-performance chemosensor for trace methotrexate and show remarkable selectivity and accuracy in complicated environmental and biological samples. The IFE-based fluorescence method is a useful strategy to fabricate simple, cost-effective, eco-friendly, and efficient fluorescent sensors for analyte detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 81271930 and 81772290), Chongqing Science and Technology Commission (CSTC2015shmszxl20097), Chongqing Graduate Student Research Innovation Project (CYB16038), Talent Introduction Project of Sichuan University of Science and Engineering(2018RCL04), the workstation in Sichuan Province GY2015-01 and sharing fund of Chongqing university's large equipment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary information Supplementary information

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.10.005.

Therapeutic drug monitoring of antimetabolic cytotoxic drugs, Brit
Evaluation of clinical assays for measuring high dose methotrexate in plasma
A preliminary study on the occurrence of cytostatic drugs in hospital effluents in beijing, China, B
High-performance liquid chromatography of methotrexate for environmental monitoring of surface contamination in hospital departments and assessment of occupational exposure
A reversed-phase high performance liquid chromatography method for quantification of methotrexate in cancer patients serum
Determination of six chemotherapeutic agents in municipal wastewater using online solid-phase extraction coupled to liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry
HPLC determination of erythrocyte methotrexate polyglutamates after low-dose methotrexate therapy in patients with rheumatoid arthritis
Flow injection method for the determination of methotrexate with a column-packed oxidizing agent
Development and validation of a turbulent flow chromatography and tandem mass spectrometry method for the quantitation of methotrexate and its metabolites 7-hydroxy methotrexate and DAMPA in serum
Determination of methotrexate in human urine at trace levels by solid phase extraction and highperformance liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry
Liquid-liquid extraction in the 96-well plate format with SRM LC MS quantitative determination of methotrexate and its major metabolite in human plasma
Flow-injection-electrochemical oxidation fluorimetry for determination of methotrexate
Four-way calibration applied to the simultaneous determination of folic acid and methotrexate in urine samples

Second-order advantage achieved with four-way fluorescence excitation-emission-kinetic data processed by parallel factor analysis and trilinear least-squares. Determination of methotrexate and leucovorin in human urine
Dihydrofolate reductase enzyme inhibition assay for plasma methotrexate determination using a 96-well microplate reader
Differential pulse adsorptive stripping voltammetric determination of nanomolar levels of methotrexate utilizing bismuth film modified electrodes
Nanocomposites of nitrogen-doped graphene decorated with a palladium silver bimetallic alloy for use as a biosensor for methotrexate detection
Highly sensitive determination of methotrexate at poly (L-lysine) modified electrode in the presence of sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate
Simultaneous electrochemical determination of epirubicin and methotrexate in human blood using a disposable electrode modified with nano-Au/MWNTs-ZnO composites
A validated chemiluminescence immunoassay for methotrexate (MTX) and its application in a pharmacokinetic study
Facile synthesis of N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon nanodots for fluorescent resonance energy transfer recognition of methotrexate with high sensitivity and selectivity
Protein-templated gold nanoclusters as fluorescence probes for the detection of methotrexate
Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
Highly biocompatible carbon nanodots for simultaneous bioimaging and targeted photodynamic therapy in vitro and in vivo
Integrating oxaliplatin with highly luminescent carbon dots: an unprecedented theranostic agent for personalized medicine
A hydrothermal route to water-stable luminescent carbon dots as nanosensors for pH and temperature
Preparation of carbon quantum dots with a high quantum yield and the application in labeling bovine serum albumin
Characterization of acid functional groups of carbon dots by nonlinear regression data fitting of potentiometric titration curves
One-step, green, and economic synthesis of water-soluble photoluminescent carbon dots by hydrothermal treatment of wheat straw, and their bio-applications in labeling, imaging, and sensing
Pentosan-derived water-soluble carbon nano dots with substantial fluorescence: properties and application as a photosensitizer
Room-temperature phosphorescence logic gates developed from nucleic acid functionalized carbon dots and graphene oxide
Correcting inner filter effects, a non multilinear tensor decomposition method
Inner filter effects and other traps in quantitative spectrofluorimetric measurements: origins and methods of correction
Gold nanocluster-assisted fluorescent detection for hydrogen peroxide and cholesterol based on the inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles
Sensitive arginine sensing based on inner filter effect of Au nanoparticles on the fluorescence of CdTe quantum dots
Upconversion nanosensor for sensitive fluorescence detection of Sudan I-IV based on inner filter effect
Sensitive fluorescent detection of melamine in raw milk based on the inner filter effect of Au nanoparticles on the fluorescence of CdTe quantum dots
Efficient inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles on the fluorescence of CdS quantum dots for sensitive detection of melamine in raw milk
Highly sensitive detection of organophosphorus pesticides represented by methamidophos via inner filter effect of Au nanoparticles on the fluorescence of CdTe quantum dots
Visual and fluorescent assays for selective detection of beta-amyloid oligomers based on the inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles on the fluorescence of CdTe quantum dots
Dopamine assay based on an aggregation-induced reversed inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles on the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots
Carbon-based dots Co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
An inner filter effect based sensor of tetracycline hydrochloride as developed by loading photoluminescent carbon nanodots in the electrospun nanofibers
Sensitive fluorescent detection of carbamate pesticides represented by methomyl based on the inner filter effect of Au nanoparticles on the fluorescence of CdTe quantum dots
Solid-phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective probing ferric ions in living cells
New iridium complex as high-efficiency red phosphorescent emitter in polymer light-emitting devices
A proof-of-concept fluorescent strategy for highly selective detection of Cr(VI) based on inner filter effect using a hydrophilic ionic chemosensor
